title: Latent degradation-state features via a fold-safe sequential filter
summary: Recent F1 work shows tire wear is better represented as a latent stochastic process with stint resets than as raw observed degradation alone.
rationale: Recent F1 work shows tire wear is better represented as a latent stochastic process with stint resets than as raw observed degradation alone. That suggests the classifier may benefit from hidden-state summaries and uncertainty, not just direct ratios.
implementation_hint: Within each driver-race sequence, residualize lap time for race context, then run a simple Kalman/state-space or exponentially updated filter that outputs current latent pace, degradation slope, shock term, and posterior uncertainty. Reset state on `PitStop==1`; use only past/current laps within fold.
expected_effect: Can separate true wear from noise/traffic and expose whether the driver is entering a high-risk stop window even when raw lap deltas are noisy.
risk: If the filter is too complex or tuned globally, gains may vanish versus cheaper lag features; wet/safety-car laps can destabilize state estimates.
disabled_reason: Disabled because an external duplicate-detection mechanism marked this hypothesis as duplicate of an existing hypothesis.
